# Questions in Livestream 14

Live Stream #14: Fourteen Pieces of Osiris

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=204" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24</a> | Should Joshua 18:15 be translated as "the spring of Merneptah"? | Yes. Falk said the usual "spring of the waters of Nephtoah" reading is weak, while the Merneptah connection makes better linguistic sense. | He argues that the Hebrew behind "waters of Nephtoah" is awkward and that reading the place name as connected to Merneptah makes better sense linguistically and historically. He treats it as a plausible trace of Egyptian contact rather than a speculative replacement for the whole passage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:14</a> | Are there Egyptian texts that speak about plague-like events, and what does he think of Titus Kennedy's use of them? | He said Middle Kingdom texts do describe events resembling the plagues, but he rejected redating those texts to the 18th dynasty. | He accepts that texts like the Admonitions tradition contain plague-like language and social upheaval that can be compared with Exodus motifs. His disagreement is with moving those Middle Kingdom texts into the 18th Dynasty; he sees them as older parallels or background, not direct 18th Dynasty reportage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:50</a> | What Exodus resources does he recommend for children aged 8 to 12? | He said there is not a good resource at that level yet, though he and his wife had tried to pitch one and may try again. | He says the gap is real: he does not know a strong Exodus resource pitched to late elementary or middle-school readers. He and his wife had tried to propose something in that space, and he leaves open the possibility of returning to it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:23</a> | Was there a decline during the reign of Thutmose II? | No. Falk said Thutmose II belongs to the New Kingdom's rise rather than its decline, even if his reign had some unusual features. | He places Thutmose II in the expanding early New Kingdom, not in a period of national decline. The reign may have been short and complicated by succession issues around Hatshepsut and Thutmose III, but that is not the same as Egypt entering decline. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=1056" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:36</a> | Was Genesis 1 written during the exile? | Probably not. He argued its core belongs much earlier, likely in the Late Bronze Age and possibly around the time of Moses. | He rejects the idea that Genesis 1 must be an exilic composition merely because it can be compared with Mesopotamian material. In his reconstruction, the core reflects much older Late Bronze Age traditions, with later transmission and updating possible but not requiring an exilic origin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=1266" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:06</a> | Why were Israelites forbidden to cut the sides of their heads, and were kosher rules partly anti-Egyptian? | He said many purity laws were mainly about making Israel a distinct people, sometimes over against Canaanite rather than Egyptian practice. | He treats the hair-cutting rule and purity laws as boundary markers that shape Israel's identity. Some rules may reject surrounding practices, but he does not reduce them to anti-Egyptian polemic; Canaanite ritual distinction and general holiness categories matter more. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=1507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:07</a> | What date does he favor for the Exodus, and does Amun-her-khepeshef's death help? | He favored about 1265 BCE and said the crown prince's death is helpful supporting evidence, though not a standalone proof. | He gives roughly 1265 BCE as his preferred date and sees the death of Ramesses II's crown prince Amun-her-khepeshef as a useful point of fit with the firstborn plague. He is careful not to make it a proof by itself, since the argument depends on a wider chronological reconstruction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=1680" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:00</a> | Are Adam and Eve meant to parallel Mesopotamian myths, or are they unique? | He said Genesis is in conversation with those myths but is not copying them; it redirects them toward its own theological claims. | He sees Genesis as participating in a shared ancient Near Eastern conversation about origins, humanity, and divine-human relations. The point is not simple copying: Genesis reshapes familiar motifs into a Yahwistic account with its own claims about creation, vocation, and sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=1892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:32</a> | If he dates the Exodus around 1260 BCE, why is Ramesses II's reign often dated 1279-1213 BCE? | He said those dates come from Kitchen's low chronology, while newer low-middle chronologies can place Ramesses II around 1288-1222 BCE. | He distinguishes the familiar Kitchen dates from the lower-middle chronology he prefers. Under that scheme, Ramesses II can begin earlier and still leave room for an Exodus around 1260-1265 BCE without forcing the event outside Ramesside chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=2039" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:59</a> | What does he think of Eric Cline's *1177 B.C.* and the Bronze Age collapse? | He accepted a collapse but said it was a long multi-stage process, not a single sharp event. | He agrees that a major eastern Mediterranean collapse happened, but cautions against treating 1177 BCE as a single decisive moment. He presents the collapse as cumulative, involving multiple pressures and regional trajectories over time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=2199" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:39</a> | If Merneptah was ruling by the time Israel was in Canaan, how does that fit the forty years in the wilderness? | He said the forty years are idiomatic rather than precise and that Israel would only need to be newly established in Canaan by Merneptah's early reign. | He reads the forty years as a conventional or rounded wilderness generation, not a stopwatch figure that must be forced into the chronology. The Merneptah Stele only requires Israel to be identifiable in Canaan by Merneptah's reign, which can still fit a late 13th-century Exodus model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=2389" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:49</a> | What is the difference between Assyriology and Egyptology, and do the two fields interact much? | They are parallel but fairly siloed disciplines with different data sets and methods, though some cross-field dialogue does happen. | He explains that Egyptology and Assyriology require different languages, corpora, and regional specializations, so scholars often train and publish in separate lanes. They overlap when chronology, diplomacy, trade, or Levantine questions require both bodies of evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=2682" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:42</a> | Is the pre-Abraham material in Genesis inspired oral history, or direct factual revelation? | He treated Genesis as inspired scripture shaped through transmission, memory, and later updating for its audience rather than simple dictation. | He does not frame inspiration as mechanical dictation of otherwise inaccessible facts. Instead, he treats early Genesis as inspired tradition that can pass through oral memory, written transmission, and later editorial shaping while still functioning as Scripture for its audience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=3027" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:27</a> | Would he rather collaborate with Niels Peter Lemche or Joel Cramer from ABR? | He said he would choose Lemche because he knows where Lemche is coming from, while his experiences with ABR have been unpleasant. | He frames the choice less as agreement with Lemche and more as predictability and professional clarity. He says he can understand Lemche's critical position, whereas his interactions with ABR have left him with serious reservations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=3130" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:10</a> | Where is the creation text where Ptah speaks creation into existence, and does that make Ptah like Yahweh? | He pointed to the Memphis Theology on the Shabaka Stone and said the similarity is real but still far from identifying Ptah with Yahweh. | He identifies the relevant text as the Memphite Theology preserved on the Shabaka Stone, where Ptah creates through heart and tongue. He accepts a meaningful comparison with speech-creation language but stresses that the theology, deity, and religious setting remain distinct from Genesis and Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=3399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:39</a> | Was Amenmesse contemporary with Seti II, and has carbon-14 ruled out some Mesopotamian chronologies? | He said Amenmesse and Seti II were contemporaries, but warned that carbon-14 is too problematic in the ancient Near East for fine absolute dating. | He treats Amenmesse and Seti II as overlapping claimants within the 19th Dynasty problem. For radiocarbon, he cautions that calibration, contamination, and regional issues make it too blunt for settling narrow ancient Near Eastern chronology disputes on its own. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=3737" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:17</a> | What should a teenage Christian expect from studying biblical theology at a Christian college? | He said it can be rewarding, but students should choose carefully because restrictive or unhealthy school culture can damage faith instead of strengthening it. | He says Christian college can be intellectually and spiritually valuable when the school is healthy and academically serious. His warning is that some environments are controlling or poorly equipped, so students should investigate faculty, culture, and fit instead of assuming the label guarantees a good formation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=4220" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:20</a> | Was temple prostitution among the Canaanites considered sacred, or mostly transactional, and how widespread was it? | He leaned toward viewing it as transactional fundraising linked to temples rather than a sacred act in itself, and probably less pervasive than often imagined. | He is cautious about broad claims of sacred prostitution. His answer leans toward temple-associated sex work as a way of generating funds or operating around cult sites, but he doubts it was as theologically central or universally pervasive as popular summaries often suggest. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=4648" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:28</a> | Which ancient Egyptian king is his favorite to study? | He especially enjoys Ramesses II, though he also highlighted Tutankhamun and Thutmose III as fascinating figures. | He names Ramesses II because of the sheer amount of material, monuments, and historical questions attached to him. Tutankhamun and Thutmose III also interest him, but Ramesses gives him the richest combination of evidence and interpretive problems. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=4808" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:08</a> | Who is his favorite Egyptian "bad guy or girl" among Set, Ammut, Apophis, or Mamra? | He chose Sekhmet, explaining that Egyptian deities were not simply good or evil and that Sekhmet's disease and healing role is especially interesting. | He pushes back on treating Egyptian deities as simple villains, then chooses Sekhmet because her role combines destructive plague or disease with healing and protection. That ambiguity makes her more interesting to him than a straightforward monster figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=5018" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:38</a> | Which was Moses's greater "bowling achievement," the Red Sea split or Sinai? | He treated the Exodus as slightly more emphasized in scripture, while still calling both events foundational. | He treats both events as central to Israel's identity: the sea crossing marks deliverance, while Sinai marks covenant formation. If forced to choose, he says Scripture tends to emphasize the Exodus deliverance slightly more, but he does not minimize Sinai. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=5441" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:41</a> | Did ancient Egyptian music have microtonal qualities similar to modern Egyptian and North African traditions? | He said probably not, though certainty is limited because ancient music was shaped by different instruments and its own tradition. | He is cautious because ancient music is difficult to reconstruct from instruments and images alone. His inclination is that modern microtonal traditions should not be projected directly backward onto pharaonic music, even though ancient Egyptian music certainly had its own distinctive sound. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=5542" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:22</a> | Were ancient Egyptian wigs only black, or were there other colored wigs? | He said surviving wigs were generally black or brownish, though original coloration can be hard to judge, and wigs also served cleanliness and perfume functions. | He says the surviving evidence points mostly to dark wigs, but preservation and original treatment make color hard to judge. He also explains that wigs were practical and social objects: they helped with cleanliness, appearance, and the use of perfumed cones or oils. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=5726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:26</a> | How did Egyptians understand the soul and the whole human person? | He said Egyptian anthropology was multi-part, including body, ka, ba, heart, shadow, and name rather than a simple body-soul scheme. | He describes Egyptian personhood as a bundle of components rather than a binary body-and-soul model. The body, ka, ba, heart, shadow, and name all mattered for identity, afterlife continuity, and ritual preservation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=6023" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:23</a> | What is the most compelling evidence for the Bronze Age historicity of the Bible? | He pointed first to Egyptian vocabulary in the Pentateuch and then to datable toponyms, while allowing for later stitching of older core materials. | He emphasizes linguistic and geographical evidence: Egyptian loanwords and names in Pentateuchal material, plus place names that fit Bronze Age settings. He allows that later editors may have stitched older material together, but sees a genuine older core behind the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=6252" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:12</a> | What DNA or genetic evidence most strongly proves the Bible's historicity? | He said DNA has been of limited use for biblical historicity because preservation is poor in Israel, though Egyptian mummification preserves more material. | He says DNA is not currently one of the strongest tools for proving biblical historicity, especially because preservation in Israel is often poor. Egypt preserves more biological material through mummification, but that does not straightforwardly answer most biblical-historical questions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/rccstKeVJoI?t=6555" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:15</a> | Does he know any good historical fiction set in Egypt? | He said no, because he does not read much historical fiction and could only offer tangential pop-culture suggestions. | He answers candidly that he is not a good source for Egyptian historical fiction recommendations because he does not read much of it. Any suggestions he can offer are more tangential pop-culture references than vetted historical-fiction picks. |
